features.summary: Delta reported Q4 2021 pre-tax profit of $170 million on revenue of $8.4 billion, with full-year revenue of $27 billion improving 67% from 2020. The company achieved second-half profitability and announced a $1,250 special profit sharing payment for employees. Omicron variant disruptions impacted operations in late December, causing $80 million in Q4 losses, but operations have since stabilized with minimal cancellations.
features.management_outlook: Management expects Q1 2022 losses in January and February before returning to profitability in March, with revenue recovery stepping down to 70% of 2019 levels from 80% in December. They remain confident in strong spring and summer travel seasons with significant pent-up demand. Full-year 2022 is expected to generate meaningful profit, with Q1 being the only loss-making quarter as the recovery resumes and accelerates.
features.risks: Omicron variant continues to impact business travel and international recovery as meetings are canceled and office reopenings postponed. Regional pilot shortages are constraining capacity in the first half of 2022, requiring 20-25% reduction in regional flying. Operational challenges and higher crew premiums are expected to impact Q1 by $60-70 million, while international markets remain constrained particularly in Asia.
features.key_drivers: Domestic leisure demand remains strong with holiday PRASM up 8% versus 2019, while business travel recovery approached 60% of 2019 levels in Q4. Premium products continue outperforming with domestic premium revenue 84% recovered, 9 points better than Main Cabin. The company gained market share among corporate customers during the pandemic and expects this to be sticky, while international capacity will remain 15-20 points below industry levels in Q1.
